title: What ambient assistants taught me about building futures
summary: An essay on ambient assistants, with one eye on the machinery and one eye on the people asked to live with it.
topic: ambient assistants after the magic show
body: I wrote this down in November 2024 after thinking about ambient assistants from the vantage point of a morning spent comparing outputs instead of admiring them. The better question, for me, was not the headline one but the one underneath it: what kind of life is this idea assuming? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. The usual sequence favors capability over context, performance over upkeep, and scale over livability. Ambient assistants seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The part that mattered to me was not the mechanism people tended to highlight in public. In public, the visible part gets all the attention, the robot, the interface, the model, the shiny layer on top. Technologies usually matter socially because of their indirect effects, not just their obvious ones. They change pacing. They change who gets to remain complex and who has to tidy themselves up for the system. They change what gets missed and what gets caught by moving the work into hidden systems. Once you notice that shift, ambient assistants stop looking like a gadget and start looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

The atmosphere around a tool is not decorative, it is part of the thing being built. Some systems create room for judgment and improvisation. Others turn ordinary life into a corridor of tiny permissions. That shift can be difficult to benchmark, but it is impossible to live inside without noticing. The mood around a system tells you something real about its politics.

Looking back from November 2024, I could already feel my own position shifting. I kept thinking about an older post of mine, “The problem with memory systems is usually the difference between an assistant and a dependency,” from September 2024. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. I was paying closer attention here to the post-demo layer: upkeep, politics, sorting logic, and the emotional contract hidden inside the workflow. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. The optimism arrived before the machinery had earned its full share of lived consequences.

I do not see withdrawal from technology as the right lesson here. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. Admiration does not require silence about the world a tool is slowly constructing around itself. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. The better habit is to hold the story modest until the evidence asks for something larger. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “I keep coming back to contactless interfaces.”

A final note to myself from November 2024: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. What holds my attention now is not the prediction itself but the burden a tool shifts onto the people nearest it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

references.title: The problem with memory systems is usually the difference between an assistant and a dependency
I keep coming back to contactless interfaces
